Use pagination when returning large API datasets to avoid memory issues.
Prefer async/await over raw promises for cleaner, more readable code.
Always validate and sanitize user input before processing it.
Use environment variables for secrets — never hardcode credentials.
Connection pooling reduces DB overhead by reusing existing connections.
Index frequently queried columns to dramatically speed up SELECT queries.
Use HTTP status codes correctly: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error.
Idempotent API endpoints (PUT, DELETE) can be safely retried without side effects.
Use a message queue (e.g. RabbitMQ, Kafka) to decouple services and handle spikes.
Rate limiting protects your API from abuse and accidental DDoS.
Use DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) to decouple your API contract from your database schema.
Soft deletes (adding an 'is_deleted' flag) preserve data integrity and support auditing.
N+1 queries are a silent killer — use JOIN or eager loading instead of querying in a loop.
Health check endpoints (/health) let load balancers and orchestrators monitor your service.
Use transactions when multiple DB writes must succeed or fail together.
Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures when a downstream service is down.
Retry logic with exponential backoff avoids hammering a struggling service.
CQRS separates read and write models, improving scalability for complex domains.
Use UUIDs instead of sequential IDs to avoid enumeration attacks.
Normalize your database to eliminate redundancy, but denormalize strategically for read performance.
Caching frequently read data (Redis, in-memory) can reduce DB load by orders of magnitude.
Use HTTPS everywhere — never transmit sensitive data over HTTP.
Log structured data (JSON) instead of plain text for easier querying and alerting.
Avoid over-engineering — solve the problem you have today, not the one you imagine tomorrow.
Use repository pattern to abstract data access logic from business logic.
Graceful shutdown ensures in-flight requests complete before your server stops.
Use a linter and formatter (ESLint, Prettier) to enforce consistent code style.
Write tests for your business logic first — it forces you to write modular, testable code.
Use dependency injection to make your code more testable and loosely coupled.
Don't trust the client — always re-validate business rules on the server side.
Versioning your API (v1, v2) lets you evolve without breaking existing consumers.
Use a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy) in front of your app server for SSL termination and load balancing.
Horizontal scaling (more instances) is usually easier than vertical scaling (bigger server).
Keep your Docker images small — use multi-stage builds and slim base images.
Use named transactions in your ORM to make rollback logic explicit and clear.
Avoid storing large files in your database — use object storage (S3, Azure Blob) instead.
Event sourcing stores every state change as an event, enabling full audit trails and replay.
Use composite indexes when queries filter on multiple columns together.
Prefer stateless services — store session state in Redis or JWTs, not in memory.
Use gzip or Brotli compression on API responses to reduce bandwidth.
Implement request tracing (correlation IDs) to track a request across multiple services.
Use feature flags to deploy code without releasing features.
Background jobs should be idempotent in case they're retried after a failure.
Always set timeouts on outbound HTTP calls to prevent hanging threads.
Use read replicas to offload heavy read queries from your primary database.
Data migrations should be backward-compatible — never rename or drop columns in one step.
Use optimistic locking to handle concurrent updates without heavy database locks.
Never log sensitive data like passwords, tokens, or PII.
Use a CDN to serve static assets closer to users.
Implement CORS correctly — only whitelist domains that actually need access.
JWT tokens should have short expiry times; use refresh tokens for long-lived sessions.
Use OpenAPI/Swagger to document your API — it becomes a contract for your consumers.
Database query plans tell you exactly why a query is slow — learn to read EXPLAIN.
Use a staging environment that mirrors production before deploying changes.
Avoid storing computed values in the database — compute them at read time or cache them.
Use bulk inserts instead of looping individual INSERTs for large datasets.
Separate your configuration from your code using a config service or environment files.
Implement proper error handling middleware to return consistent error response shapes.
Use dead letter queues to catch and inspect messages that failed processing.
Service discovery lets microservices find each other dynamically without hardcoded URLs.
Use partial indexes to index only the subset of rows you actually query.
Prefer query builders or parameterized queries over string concatenation to prevent SQL injection.
Use a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) instead of storing secrets in .env files.
Blue-green deployments let you switch traffic between two identical environments with zero downtime.
Use structured logging levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR — and filter by level in production.
Implement API timeouts at the gateway level as a safety net for all services.
Database connection strings should never appear in source code or logs.
Use server-sent events or WebSockets for real-time data instead of polling.
Prefer immutable data structures where possible to reduce bugs from unexpected mutation.
Use a task scheduler (cron, node-cron) for periodic jobs instead of long-running loops.
Apply the principle of least privilege — services should only access what they need.
Use query caching carefully — stale cache can be worse than no cache.
Domain events decouple side effects (sending email, updating stats) from core business logic.
Use an API gateway to handle auth, rate limiting, and routing in one place.
Write migration scripts that can be run up and down for safe rollbacks.
Use prepared statements to improve both security and query performance.
Monorepos simplify dependency sharing across multiple services in a large codebase.
Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets instead of OFFSET — it scales much better.
Always define explicit column names in SELECT instead of using SELECT *.
Use Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes to control traffic to healthy pods only.
Implement request queuing at peak load rather than rejecting requests outright.
Use a consistent naming convention across your API — camelCase or snake_case, pick one.
Apply database constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FK) — don't rely solely on app-level validation.
Use async message processing for operations that don't need an immediate response.
Containerizing your app ensures it runs the same way in dev, staging, and production.
Use ETag headers for HTTP caching to avoid sending unchanged responses.
Prefer small, focused microservices that own one domain over large services that do everything.
Use a schema registry when working with Kafka to version and validate message schemas.
Implement request deduplication for payment or order endpoints to prevent double processing.
Use connection retries with jitter when connecting to external services at startup.
Apply the open/closed principle — your code should be open for extension, closed for modification.
Use database views to simplify complex queries that are reused across the codebase.
Run your database migrations as part of your deployment pipeline, not manually.
Use a reverse proxy cache (Nginx, Varnish) for heavily-read, rarely-changing endpoints.
Track slow queries using your database's slow query log and optimize them proactively.
Use content negotiation (Accept headers) to support multiple response formats (JSON, XML).
Implement API deprecation notices via response headers before removing old endpoints.
Use distributed tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin) to visualize request flows across microservices.
Keep your business logic in the service layer, not in controllers or repositories.
Use database partitioning for very large tables to improve query performance.
Avoid premature optimization — profile first, then optimize the actual bottleneck.
Use a consistent error schema (code, message, details) across all your API endpoints.
Implement webhook signature verification to ensure incoming events are authentic.
Use long polling as a simpler alternative to WebSockets for low-frequency real-time updates.
Apply the single responsibility principle — each class or function should do one thing well.
Use database sharding to horizontally scale your database beyond a single machine.
Implement exponential backoff in your retry logic to avoid thundering herd problems.
Use async queues for sending emails, notifications, and other non-critical side effects.
Monitor your queue depth — a growing queue is an early warning of a processing bottleneck.
Use column-level encryption for highly sensitive fields like SSNs or payment data.
Prefer explicit transactions over implicit ones for clarity and correctness.
Use canary deployments to test new versions with a small percentage of real traffic.
Apply the 12-factor app methodology for building scalable, maintainable backend services.
Use a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) for secure, observable service-to-service communication.
Keep your API responses consistent — always include the same fields, even if null.
Use bulk operations in your ORM to reduce the number of round trips to the database.
Implement proper GDPR/data-privacy handling: support data deletion and export requests.
Use chaos engineering to proactively find weaknesses before they cause production incidents.
Apply backpressure in streaming systems to prevent fast producers from overwhelming slow consumers.
Use materialized views to precompute expensive queries and refresh them on a schedule.
Implement API key rotation without downtime using a grace period for old keys.
Use write-ahead logging (WAL) in PostgreSQL for durability and replication.
Always handle the case where an external API call returns unexpected data.
Use a bulkhead pattern to isolate failures in one part of your system from others.
Apply event-driven architecture to improve resilience and decoupling between services.
Use database seeding scripts to set up consistent test and development environments.
Implement multi-tenancy at the schema, database, or row level depending on your isolation needs.
Use shadow mode to run a new service in parallel with the old one before switching traffic.
Avoid chatty APIs — design endpoints that return everything the client needs in one call.
Use a task queue (Celery, Bull) to move CPU-intensive work off your main request thread.
Profile your application under realistic load before going to production.
Use lazy loading in your ORM to avoid loading unnecessary relationships by default.
Implement HATEOAS to make your REST API self-discoverable.
Use database triggers sparingly — they can make debugging very difficult.
Apply the saga pattern to manage distributed transactions across microservices.
Use an outbox pattern to reliably publish events after a database write without dual-write issues.
Keep your controller thin — it should only parse input, call a service, and return output.
Use a data lake for storing raw events cheaply before processing and structuring them.
Implement graceful degradation — if a non-critical service fails, keep the core working.
Use API mocking in tests to isolate units and avoid hitting real external services.
Apply SOLID principles consistently to keep your codebase maintainable as it grows.
Use database explain analyze (not just explain) to get actual runtime stats for queries.
Implement token revocation using a deny list stored in Redis for immediate invalidation.
Use a message broker for guaranteed delivery instead of direct HTTP calls between services.
Apply defensive programming — never assume external input is in the expected format.
Use cache invalidation strategies (TTL, event-based) that match your data's update frequency.
Monitor p95/p99 latencies, not just averages — tail latencies reveal the worst user experience.
Implement pessimistic locking when conflicting updates are frequent and must be prevented.
Use read-through caching to automatically populate the cache on a cache miss.
Apply the strangler fig pattern to gradually replace a legacy system with a new one.
Use immutable infrastructure — replace servers instead of updating them in place.
Implement multi-region deployment for high availability and disaster recovery.
Use write-behind caching to batch database writes for improved throughput.
Apply the ambassador pattern to handle cross-cutting concerns like auth and logging in a proxy.
Use event replay to rebuild read models or migrate data to a new format.
Implement zero-downtime database migrations using expand-contract pattern.
Use consistent hashing to distribute load evenly across cache nodes.
Apply the sidecar pattern to attach helper processes (logging, monitoring) to your service.
Use a time-series database (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for metrics and monitoring data.
Implement API response envelopes for consistency: { data, meta, errors }.
Use database connection pooling with a max pool size appropriate for your server's resources.
Apply the aggregator pattern to combine responses from multiple services into one.
Use GraphQL subscriptions for real-time data when you're already using GraphQL.
Implement proper TLS certificate management and auto-renewal with Let's Encrypt.
Use a service registry (Consul, Eureka) for dynamic service discovery in microservices.
Apply the two-phase commit protocol carefully — it's slow and complex, use sagas instead.
Use database streaming for processing large result sets without loading them into memory.
Implement rate limiting per user, per IP, and per endpoint for fine-grained control.
Use a proxy cache like Nginx for caching responses from your application server.
Apply the backends for frontends (BFF) pattern to tailor APIs to specific client needs.
Use a distributed lock (Redis Redlock) to prevent concurrent execution of critical sections.
Implement request coalescing to combine multiple identical requests into one upstream call.
Use database replication lag monitoring to detect when replicas fall behind.
Apply the throttle pattern to limit resource consumption per user or tenant.
Use async database drivers to prevent blocking your event loop in Node.js applications.
Implement proper cascading deletes at the database level, not just in application code.
Use a content addressable storage approach for deduplicating file uploads.
Apply the compensating transaction pattern to undo completed steps in a failed saga.
Use connection draining when shutting down service instances to avoid dropping requests.
Implement proper HTTP caching with Cache-Control headers to reduce load on your API.
Use a search engine (Elasticsearch, Meilisearch) for full-text search instead of LIKE queries.
Apply the scatter-gather pattern to fan out requests and aggregate results.
Use database hot standby for fast failover with minimal data loss.
Implement request hedging — send duplicate requests to multiple servers and use the fastest.
Use column store databases for analytics workloads requiring aggregation across many rows.
Apply the anti-corruption layer pattern when integrating with legacy systems.
Use advisory locks in PostgreSQL for application-level mutual exclusion.
Implement proper 4xx vs 5xx error handling — 4xx is the client's fault, 5xx is yours.
Use a workflow engine (Temporal, Conductor) for long-running, multi-step business processes.
Apply the cache-aside pattern to manually manage what gets cached and when.
Use partial responses (sparse fieldsets) to let clients request only the fields they need.
Implement change data capture (CDC) to stream database changes to other systems.
Use a graph database (Neo4j) for data with complex, deeply nested relationships.
Apply the proxy pattern to add behavior (auth, logging) transparently to a service.
Use connection timeouts separate from read timeouts — they solve different failure modes.
Implement webhook retries with exponential backoff and a dead letter queue for failures.
Use server-side cursors for iterating large datasets without loading everything into memory.
Apply the correlation ID pattern to link logs across services for a single user request.
Use database connection health checks to detect and remove stale connections from the pool.
Implement proper multipart file upload handling with size and type validation.
Use a policy engine (OPA, Casbin) for complex authorization rules instead of if-else chains.
Apply the retry budget pattern to prevent too many retries from overwhelming a service.
Use point-in-time recovery (PITR) for databases to restore to any moment in time.
Implement audit logging for all write operations in sensitive domains.
Use a cache warming strategy to pre-populate caches after a deployment.
Apply the command pattern to encapsulate operations as objects for queuing and logging.
Use database query hints sparingly — they can make the optimizer's job harder.
Implement proper ACID compliance verification in your transaction-heavy workflows.
Use a feature store for managing and serving machine learning features at scale.
Apply the claim check pattern to pass large payloads by reference instead of by value.
Use query result streaming to send data to clients as it arrives from the database.
Implement database query whitelisting in production to prevent arbitrary query execution.
Use a scheduler with lock (e.g. quartz with a DB lock) to prevent duplicate job runs in a cluster.
Apply the API facade pattern to simplify complex subsystems behind a clean interface.
Use document databases (MongoDB) for highly variable or nested data structures.
Implement proper back-pressure handling in your streaming pipelines.
Use rolling deployments to update instances gradually and detect issues early.
Apply the chain of responsibility pattern to process requests through a pipeline of handlers.
Use database query parameterization even for non-user-input values as a best practice.
Implement proper session fixation prevention by regenerating session IDs after login.
Use multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) databases for high-read, high-write workloads.
Apply the mediator pattern to reduce direct dependencies between components.
Use a dedicated metrics server (Prometheus, Datadog) instead of logging metrics as text.
Implement lazy initialization for expensive resources to improve startup time.
Use database row-level security to enforce data access policies at the database level.
Apply the façade pattern to wrap legacy code in a clean, modern interface.
Use token bucket or leaky bucket algorithms for smooth rate limiting.
Implement proper OAuth 2.0 flows — client credentials for service-to-service, PKCE for SPAs.
Use database archiving to move old data to cold storage and keep tables lean.
Apply the specification pattern to encapsulate business rules as composable conditions.
Use request signing (HMAC) to verify the integrity and authenticity of API requests.
Implement read-your-writes consistency to avoid confusing users after they submit data.
Use a data mesh architecture to treat data as a product owned by domain teams.
Apply the gateway aggregation pattern to reduce client round trips in microservice architectures.
Use database statement timeouts to kill long-running queries before they impact performance.
Implement proper multitenancy isolation testing to ensure one tenant can't access another's data.
Use a log aggregation platform (ELK, Loki) to centralize and search logs across services.
Apply the observer pattern to notify multiple components of state changes without coupling.
Use write amplification awareness when designing storage systems for SSDs.
Implement proper backfill strategies when adding new columns to large tables.
Use a graph of trust for establishing service-to-service authentication via mTLS.
Apply the pipes and filters pattern to build composable data processing pipelines.
Use database foreign key constraints to enforce referential integrity at the database level.
Implement proper CORS preflight caching to reduce unnecessary OPTIONS requests.
Use leader election (Raft, ZooKeeper) to coordinate distributed tasks with a single master.
Apply the decorator pattern to add behavior to existing objects without changing their class.
Use columnar storage (Parquet, ORC) for efficient analytical queries on large datasets.
Implement proper secret rotation without downtime using versioned secrets.
Use a shared-nothing architecture to maximize horizontal scalability.
Apply the priority queue pattern to process high-priority tasks before low-priority ones.
Use database JSON columns carefully — they're flexible but not as fast as normalized columns.
Implement proper end-to-end encryption for highly sensitive data in transit and at rest.
Use a query cache invalidation strategy based on entity types to avoid stale data.
Apply the composite pattern to treat individual objects and collections uniformly.
Use a database proxy (PgBouncer, ProxySQL) to pool connections at the infrastructure level.
Implement proper graceful shutdown hooks to close DB connections and flush queues cleanly.
Use tail-based sampling in distributed tracing to keep traces for slow or failed requests.
Apply the adapter pattern to make incompatible interfaces work together.
Use database sequences instead of MAX(id)+1 to avoid race conditions in ID generation.
Implement proper input length validation to prevent resource exhaustion attacks.
Use a dedicated config server (Spring Cloud Config, Consul) for centralized configuration.
Apply the flyweight pattern to reduce memory usage for large numbers of similar objects.
Use database index-only scans by ensuring your index covers all selected columns.
Implement proper API versioning deprecation timelines — give consumers at least 6 months.
Use a circuit breaker dashboard to visualize the health of inter-service dependencies.
Apply the template method pattern to define a skeleton algorithm with customizable steps.
Use database ANALYZE to update statistics and help the query planner make better decisions.
Implement proper multi-factor authentication for admin and sensitive user accounts.
Use a distributed cache with replication to avoid a single point of failure.
Apply the strategy pattern to swap algorithms or behaviors at runtime.
Use database triggers only for cross-cutting concerns that must always be enforced.
Implement proper API contract testing (Pact) to catch breaking changes before deployment.
Use a dedicated service for sending notifications to decouple it from your core business logic.
Apply the iterator pattern to provide a uniform way to traverse different data structures.
Use database hot-warm-cold storage tiers to balance cost and performance for aging data.
Implement proper load testing before every major release using tools like k6 or Locust.
Use a schema migration tool (Flyway, Liquibase) to version-control your database schema.
Apply the state machine pattern for managing complex lifecycle transitions in your domain.
Use database full-text search indexes (GIN in PostgreSQL) for text search queries.
Implement proper certificate pinning for mobile apps connecting to your backend.
Use a blue-green or canary strategy for zero-downtime database schema migrations.
Apply the visitor pattern to add operations to object structures without modifying them.
Use database query tagging to identify which application code generated a slow query.
Implement proper idempotency keys for payment APIs to prevent duplicate charges.
Use a dedicated logging library (Winston, Serilog) instead of console.log in production.
Apply the null object pattern to avoid repetitive null checks throughout your code.
Use database connection string rotation to improve security without redeploying your app.
Implement proper multi-cloud strategy to avoid vendor lock-in for critical infrastructure.
Use asynchronous task processing for anything that takes more than 200ms to complete.
Apply the prototype pattern to clone complex objects efficiently.
Use database covering indexes to answer queries entirely from the index without a table scan.
Implement proper error budgets to balance reliability work with feature development.
Use a dedicated secrets backend instead of environment variables for production deployments.
Apply the builder pattern to construct complex objects step by step.
Use database explain buffers to understand cache hit rates during query execution.
Implement proper graceful handling of downstream service version mismatches.
Use a monorepo with independent deployability for teams that share a lot of code.
Apply the factory method pattern to decouple object creation from its usage.
Use database RETURNING clauses to get inserted/updated rows without an extra SELECT.
Implement proper API authentication for webhooks to prevent spoofed events.
Use a dedicated error tracking service (Sentry, Rollbar) to monitor and triage exceptions.
Apply the abstract factory pattern to create families of related objects.
Use database query batching to reduce round trips when fetching related data.
Implement proper cache stampede protection using probabilistic early expiration.
Use a traffic shadowing technique to test new services under real production load safely.
Apply the singleton pattern only when truly necessary — it introduces global state.
Use database hot spot detection to identify and resolve contention on heavily written rows.
Implement proper distributed rate limiting using a shared Redis store across instances.
Use a domain-driven design bounded context to keep your models and language consistent.
Apply the service locator pattern with caution — it can hide dependencies and hurt testability.
Use database partial updates (PATCH) to avoid overwriting fields you didn't intend to change.
Implement proper async error boundaries to prevent unhandled rejections from crashing your app.
Use a write-optimized data structure (LSM tree) for workloads with very high write throughput.
Apply event-carried state transfer to avoid chatty service-to-service lookups.
Use database trigger-based auditing as a fallback when application-level auditing is bypassed.
Implement proper blue-green DNS switching with short TTLs for fast and safe cutover.
Use a read model tailored to your UI needs instead of returning raw domain objects.
Apply test-driven development (TDD) to catch bugs early and improve your design.
Use database connection validation queries (SELECT 1) to detect and discard stale connections.
Implement proper zero-trust networking — authenticate and authorize every service call.
Use an API changelog to communicate every breaking and non-breaking change to consumers.
Apply infrastructure as code (Terraform, Pulumi) to version-control your cloud resources.
Use database warm caches by running common queries after a deployment before sending traffic.
Implement proper canary analysis using automated metrics comparison before full rollout.
Use a saga orchestrator for complex workflows where you need centralized error handling.
Apply the hexagonal architecture to keep your domain logic independent of frameworks and databases.
Use database event notifications (LISTEN/NOTIFY in PostgreSQL) for lightweight pub/sub.
Implement proper observability with the three pillars: logs, metrics, and traces.
Use a query optimization loop: measure, identify bottlenecks, optimize, verify, repeat.
Always write code as if the next person to maintain it is a tired version of yourself.
Use database connection multiplexing to serve more clients with fewer server connections.
Implement proper request body size limits to prevent memory exhaustion attacks.
Use a write buffer to batch small writes into larger ones for better I/O throughput.
Apply the YAGNI principle — don't add functionality until it's actually needed.
Use database hot backup tools (pg_basebackup) for consistent backups without downtime.
Implement proper API authentication token storage — never in localStorage for sensitive apps.
Use a service catalog to document all available internal APIs and their owners.
Apply the DRY principle — but don't over-abstract; duplication is better than the wrong abstraction.
Use database query result streaming to serve large exports without buffering in memory.
Implement proper semantic versioning (semver) for your APIs and packages.
Use a chaos monkey to randomly kill services in staging and verify your system recovers.
Apply the principle of progressive disclosure in your API design — simple defaults, optional power.
Use database hot table partitioning to archive old rows without affecting active data.
Implement proper mutual TLS (mTLS) for service-to-service authentication in production.
Use a load shedder to drop low-priority requests under extreme load and protect core flows.
Apply the fail-fast principle — detect configuration or dependency errors at startup, not runtime.
Use database index bloat monitoring to detect indexes that need rebuilding.
Implement proper idempotency across your entire API surface, not just payment endpoints.
Use a contract-first API design approach — define the schema before writing any code.
Apply the bounded retry pattern to limit total retry attempts across a request's lifecycle.
Use database query normalization to ensure equivalent queries hit the same query plan cache entry.
Implement proper cross-region replication for disaster recovery with defined RPO and RTO.
Use a deployment pipeline with automated smoke tests to catch regressions immediately.
Apply the interface segregation principle — clients should not depend on interfaces they don't use.
Use database autovacuum tuning to prevent table bloat and maintain query performance.
Implement proper API documentation with real request/response examples, not just schemas.
Use a centralized configuration with hot reload to change settings without redeploying.
Apply the Liskov substitution principle — subtypes must be substitutable for their base types.
Use database statement-level replication for fine-grained control over what gets replicated.
Implement proper SLA monitoring and alerting so you know before your customers do.
Use a pre-commit hook to run linting and tests before every commit.
Apply the Dependency Inversion principle — depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations.
Use database query federation to query multiple databases as if they were one.
Implement proper capacity planning by tracking resource usage trends, not just current usage.
Review your code with fresh eyes after 24 hours — you'll always find something to improve.
